MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Defendant Kaiser Gypsum Company, Inc.
Off Calendar Sep-25-2014 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Amber R. Craig of DEHAY ELLISTON LLP (510-285-0748), appearing for defendant / moving party Kaiser Gypsum Company, Inc.
Kimberly J. Chu of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Elaine Percy, Steven Steele, et al.

RULING:
DEFENDANT KAISER GYPSUM COMPANY, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Kaiser Gypsum Company, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to demonstrate that all of plaintiffs' claims against it fail even if defendant owed no duty to Elaine Percy. Duty is not an element of strict products liability. (Elsheref v. Applied Materials, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 451.) Defendant moved solely for summary judgment and did not include an alternate request for summary adjudication of individual causes of action. The Court further denied defendant's oral request for a 10-day extension to file a writ per C.C.P. 437c(m)(1). Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Early K. Langley of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #3537 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Apr-15-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Michelle C. Golden of PRINDLE, AMARO, GOETZ, HILLYARD, BARNES & REINHOLTZ, LLP (415-788-8354), appearing for defendant / moving party Frederick Meiswinkel, Inc.

RULING:
DEFENDANT FREDERICK MEISWINKEL, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, ALTERNATIVELY, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

No other appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Frederick Meiswinkel, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendant sustained its initial burden and plaintiffs did not produce admissible evidence that defendant knew or reasonably should have known that the installation, use, handling and/or disturbance of asbestos-containing products or materials could create a safety hazard of secondary exposure for a member of a household of a non-employee. Even if decedent's injury was foreseeable, pursuant to Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108 and Campbell v. Ford Motor Company (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th15, public policy dictates against a creation of a duty in the present matter. Plaintiffs initially did not oppose the portion of defendant's motion arguing plaintiffs cannot prevail on their products liability causes of action. Assuming without deciding that the court can consider the new arguments and evidence provided in plaintiffs' supplemental briefing, plaintiffs did not create a triable issue whether defendant is liable under a theory of strict products liability. Elsheref v. Applied Materials, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 451 is inapplicable to the strict products liability issue here. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Matter not reported. (503/TLJ)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Westburne Supply Inc.S
Off Calendar Jan-29-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Pamela Stevens of WALSWORTH FRANKLIN BEVINS & McCALL, LLP (415-781-7072), appearing for defendant / moving party Westburne Supply, Inc.
James P. Nevin of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties - Elaine Percy / Steven Steele, et al.

RULING:
DEFENDANT WESTBURNE SUPPLY, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Westburne Supply Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs' claims against it are barred by the statute of limitations, C.C.P. 340.2. Plaintiffs' First Amended Complaint for survival, wrongful death was filed within the statute of limitations period and defendant failed to demonstrate that the amendment substituting it into this action does not relate back to the filing of the First Amended Complaint. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Tara A. Lohman of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #13428 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Rpi Companys Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Jan-08-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Robert M. Menchini of FOLEY & MANSFIELD (510-590-9500), appearing for defendant / moving party RPI Company
Oren P. Noah of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Elaine Percy/ Steven Steele, et al.

RULING:
DEFENDANT RPI COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant RPI Company's motion for summary judgment is denied. The deposition testimony of Merle Steele and Elaine Percy, the Social Security earnings statement of Merle Steele and Dr. Richard Cohen's declaration create a triable issue whether defendant owed a duty to Elaine Percy. Specifically, they create a triable issue whether defendant knew or reasonably should have known that the installation, removal, use, disturbance and/or sweeping of asbestos-containing products or materials could create a safety hazard of secondary exposure for a member of an employee's household. Pursuant to Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108 and Campbell v. Ford Motor Company (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th15, public policy dictates in favor of a creation of a duty in the present matter given that Merle Steele was employed by Plant Insulation. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Tara A. Lohman of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #13428 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)

DEFENDANT N.V. HEATHORN, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

The respective counsel stipulated to argue both defendants' motions at the same time. Defendant N.V. Heathorn, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendant sustained its initial burden and plaintiffs did not produce admissible evidence that defendant knew or reasonably should have known that the disturbance of asbestos-containing material could create a safety hazard of secondary exposure for a member of a household of a non-employee. Even if decedent's injury was foreseeable, pursuant to Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108 and Campbell v. Ford Motor Company (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th15, public policy dictates against a creation of a duty in the present matter. Plaintiffs have dismissed their strict liability cause of action. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Miles Lendway of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #13684 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)

Off Calendar Mar-05-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Jon Eldredge of GLYNN & FINLEY, LLP (925-210-2805) appearing for defendant / moving party Pacific Bell Telephone Company
Jennifer L. Alesio of BRAYTON & PURCELL (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Elaine Percy / Steven Steel, et al.

RULING:
DEFENDANT PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Pacific Bell Telephone Company's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is granted as to issues 1 and 3 (negligence) and denied as to issues 2 and 4 (strict liability). As to the negligence causes of action, defendant sustained its initial burden and plaintiffs did not produce admissible evidence that defendant knew or reasonably should have known that the cleaning up, sweeping and/or disturbance of asbestos-containing material could create a safety hazard of secondary exposure for a member of a household of a non-employee. Even if decedent's injury was foreseeable, pursuant to Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108 and Campbell v. Ford Motor Company (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th15, public policy dictates against a creation of a duty in the present matter. As to the strict liability causes of action, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden under either prong of Aguilar. Defendant failed to present evidence negating an essential element of plaintiffs' strict liability causes of action. Defendant also failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence to support their strict liability causes of action. By failing to demonstrate that it propounded contention discovery designed to elicit all facts and evidence plaintiffs have to support their claims for strict liability, and by failing to demonstrate that plaintiffs submitted factually devoid responses thereto, defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Patricia Rodriguez of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #12436 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)
